title: A Typical Day

story.location: Carlos and Marta’s apartment and the nearby neighborhood
story.time: Saturday afternoon while the rain eases
story.scene: The kettle is on and rain still taps the apartment windows. Carlos uses the familiar rooms and objects around him to describe an ordinary weekday, then stops being the example and asks the learner to describe their own day.
story.mission: Describe four parts of a normal day in order using a small set of first-person present-tense verbs.
story.continuity: The scene begins exactly where Lesson 8 ends. The apartment returns as a recurring landmark, but the communication goal shifts from describing space to describing life inside it.

emotionalArc.startsWith: The learner can describe Carlos’s surroundings but may not yet believe everyday Spanish belongs in their own life.
emotionalArc.endsWith: The learner can tell Carlos what they normally do from morning to night.
emotionalArc.earnedBy: Turning a few repeated daily actions into a connected, personal account rather than memorizing a full verb table.

microCliffhanger.speaker: Carlos
microCliffhanger.spanish: Ya no llueve. Mañana hacemos un día completo por Madrid. Esta vez, tú tomas las decisiones.
microCliffhanger.english: It isn’t raining anymore. Tomorrow we’ll spend a full day around Madrid. This time, you make the decisions.

carlosIntroduction.title: Tell me about your day
carlosIntroduction.message: You’ve spent the day responding to me. Now I want to hear about you. I’ll show you how I describe a normal weekday using a few simple yo forms. Then you’ll connect four real parts of your own day—from morning to night.
carlosIntroduction.prompt: Begin with: Por la mañana, desayuno...

canDo: Name four common parts of the day.
Use a small set of regular yo forms ending in -o.
Put daily actions in a clear order.
Describe a simple personal routine from morning to night.
objectives: Recognize Carlos’s daily routine at natural and slow speed.
Use desayuno, trabajo or estudio, como, and descanso as first-person actions.
Connect actions with primero, después, luego, and al final.
Personalize a four-part routine without reading a full model.

recycledLanguage.use: Show who shares Carlos’s everyday routine.
Move familiar food language into an ordinary home routine.
Anchor the routine in the apartment the learner just explored.

essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Todos los días trabajo por la mañana.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: Every day I work in the morning.

essentialPhrases.usage: Begin the first part of a day.
essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Por la mañana, desayuno en casa.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: In the morning, I have breakfast at home.

essentialPhrases.usage: Move into the middle of the day.

essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Por la tarde, camino por el barrio.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: In the afternoon, I walk through the neighborhood.

essentialPhrases.usage: Describe the final part of the day.
essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Por la noche, ceno con mi familia.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: At night, I have dinner with my family.

essentialPhrases.usage: Connect one action to the next.
Describe what usually happens without claiming it happens every day.
essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Normalmente practico español por la tarde.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: I normally practice Spanish in the afternoon.

essentialPhrases.usage: Ask another person about their routine as a useful fixed question.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: What do you normally do in the morning?

learnerChoices.objective: State one genuine study-time preference that can help Carlos personalize future practice.

options.label: Morning
options.learnerEnglish: I practice Spanish in the morning.
options.carlosSpanish: Buena elección. Por la mañana la mente suele estar fresca. Podemos empezar el día con una conversación corta.
options.carlosEnglish: Good choice. The mind is often fresh in the morning. We can start the day with a short conversation.
options.modelOrder: Por la mañana, desayuno y practico español.
options.modelOrderEnglish: In the morning, I have breakfast and practice Spanish.

options.label: Afternoon
options.learnerEnglish: I practice Spanish in the afternoon.
options.carlosSpanish: Buena elección. Una práctica corta por la tarde puede separar el trabajo del resto del día.
options.carlosEnglish: Good choice. A short afternoon practice can separate work from the rest of the day.
options.modelOrder: Por la tarde, camino y practico español.
options.modelOrderEnglish: In the afternoon, I walk and practice Spanish.

options.label: Evening
options.learnerEnglish: I practice Spanish in the evening.
options.carlosSpanish: Buena elección. Por la noche puedes repasar el español que has usado durante el día.
options.carlosEnglish: Good choice. In the evening you can review the Spanish you used during the day.
options.modelOrder: Por la noche, practico español y descanso.
options.modelOrderEnglish: In the evening, I practice Spanish and rest.

learnerChoices.rejoin: Carlos acknowledges the chosen practice time, then asks for the learner’s complete morning-to-night routine.

vocabulary.tip: Use this only for something genuinely regular.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Todos los días desayuno en casa.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Every day I have breakfast at home.

vocabulary.tip: Use por with a broad part of the day.
Treat al mediodía as one useful time chunk.
A familiar phrase from making plans now organizes a routine.
Spanish uses noche for both evening and night contexts.
Trabajo temprano means I work early.
Useful when a routine is typical but not absolute.
Use it to make a routine sound honest and flexible.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: A veces trabajo por la tarde.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Sometimes I work in the afternoon.

vocabulary.tip: From desayunar; the yo form ends in -o.
From trabajar; context distinguishes the verb from el trabajo, work or job.
From estudiar; keep the three syllables clear.
From practicar; the c keeps the hard k sound before o.
Carlos uses this for preparing his classes.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I prepare my classes at home.

vocabulary.tip: Without an accent, como can be the yo form of comer; context makes the meaning clear.
From caminar; reuse the action from walking through Madrid.
From cenar; Spanish distinguishes breakfast, lunch, and dinner verbs.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I have dinner with my family.

vocabulary.tip: From descansar; a useful final action in a routine.
From hablar; the h is silent.
Use it to make the first step unmistakable.
The written accent marks the stressed final syllable.
Alternate it with después to avoid repetition.
Place it before the verb for a simple beginner pattern.
Use it to close the routine.
Rutina is feminine: la rutina.
Día is masculine despite ending in -a.
Do not add la in this fixed phrase.

vocabulary.english: away from home / outside the home
vocabulary.tip: Useful for work, school, or meals outside.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: A veces como fuera de casa.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Sometimes I eat away from home.

vocabulary.tip: Learn this as one fixed question; full hacer conjugation comes later.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: What do you normally do in the afternoon?

grammar.meaning: Say what you normally do using a small set of useful yo forms.
grammar.explanation: For the regular verbs in this lesson, remove -ar or -er and add -o when talking about yourself: trabajar → trabajo, practicar → practico, comer → como. Spanish usually omits yo because the -o ending already shows who is speaking. This lesson uses the forms as practical chunks; it does not teach a full conjugation table.

notes.title: -AR verbs
notes.text: desayunar → desayuno; trabajar → trabajo; descansar → descanso

notes.title: Skip yo naturally
notes.text: Trabajo por la mañana sounds complete without Yo.

notes.title: Keep the scope small
notes.text: Describe your own routine only. Other subject forms come later.

examples.spanish: Por la mañana, desayuno y trabajo.
examples.english: In the morning, I have breakfast and work.
At midday, I eat at home.
In the afternoon, I practice Spanish.

examples.spanish: Por la noche, ceno y descanso.
examples.english: At night, I have dinner and rest.

grammar.practicePrompt: Choose four real actions and connect them from morning to night.

dialogue.title: A normal day with Carlos
dialogue.setting: Tea is ready in the apartment. Carlos describes his weekday, then asks the learner to take over.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: La lluvia sigue. Mientras esperamos, te cuento mi rutina.
lines.english: The rain continues. While we wait, I’ll tell you about my routine.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Por la mañana, desayuno con Marta y Nico. Después, trabajo y preparo mis clases.
lines.english: In the morning, I have breakfast with Marta and Nico. Afterwards, I work and prepare my classes.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Al mediodía, como en casa. Por la tarde, camino por el barrio.
lines.english: At midday, I eat at home. In the afternoon, I walk through the neighborhood.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Por la noche, ceno con mi familia y descanso.
lines.english: At night, I have dinner with my family and rest.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: When do you normally practice Spanish?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: I practice Spanish in the morning.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Ahora cuéntame tu día. ¿Qué haces por la mañana?
lines.english: Now tell me about your day. What do you do in the morning?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Por la mañana, desayuno. Después, trabajo. Por la tarde, practico español. Por la noche, descanso.
lines.english: In the morning, I have breakfast. Afterwards, I work. In the afternoon, I practice Spanish. At night, I rest.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Perfecto. Ya puedes contar un día completo en español.
lines.english: Perfect. You can now describe a complete day in Spanish.

comprehension.prompt: What does Carlos do after breakfast?
What does Carlos ask the learner to describe?

listening.title: Carlos’s weekday
listening.instructions: Listen, follow along, then check what you understood.
listening.naturalScript: Normalmente desayuno con mi familia. Después, trabajo y preparo mis clases. Al mediodía, como en casa. Por la tarde, camino por el barrio. Por la noche, ceno con Marta y Nico y descanso.
listening.slowScript: Normalmente / desayuno con mi familia. / Después, / trabajo / y preparo mis clases. / Al mediodía, / como en casa. / Por la tarde, / camino por el barrio. / Por la noche, / ceno con Marta y Nico / y descanso.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.english: I normally have breakfast with my family.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.spanish: Después, trabajo y preparo mis clases.
transcript.english: Afterwards, I work and prepare my classes.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.english: At midday, I eat at home.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.spanish: Por la tarde, camino por el barrio.
transcript.english: In the afternoon, I walk through the neighborhood.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.spanish: Por la noche, ceno con Marta y Nico y descanso.
transcript.english: At night, I have dinner with Marta and Nico and rest.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.english: When do you normally practice Spanish?

transcript.speaker: Learner
transcript.english: I practice Spanish in the morning.

comprehension.explanation: Desayuno con mi familia means I have breakfast with my family.

comprehension.prompt: What does Carlos do after breakfast?
comprehension.explanation: Después, trabajo y preparo mis clases describes the next part of his day.

comprehension.prompt: When does Carlos walk through the neighborhood?
comprehension.explanation: Por la tarde means in the afternoon.

comprehension.prompt: What does Carlos ask the learner about?
comprehension.explanation: ¿Cuándo practicas español normalmente? asks when the learner normally practices Spanish.

items.spanish: Por la mañana, desayuno con mi familia.
options: In the morning, I have breakfast with my family.
In the afternoon, I walk alone.
items.answer: In the morning, I have breakfast with my family.

items.spanish: Después, trabajo y preparo mis clases.
options: Afterwards, I work and prepare my classes.
Sometimes, I study at the café.
items.answer: Afterwards, I work and prepare my classes.

options: At midday, I eat at home.
Every morning, I work at home.
items.answer: At midday, I eat at home.

items.spanish: Por la tarde, camino por el barrio.
options: In the afternoon, I walk through the neighborhood.
In the morning, I study Spanish.
At midday, I visit the market.
items.answer: In the afternoon, I walk through the neighborhood.

items.spanish: Por la noche, ceno y descanso.
options: At night, I have dinner and rest.
At night, I work and study.
In the afternoon, I have breakfast.
items.answer: At night, I have dinner and rest.

pronunciation.focus: A clear -o ending and the rhythm of time phrase plus action
tips: Keep the final o short and clear in trabajo, como, and descanso.
Stress -día in mediodía and -pués in después.
Pause briefly after each time phrase to make the routine easy to follow.
The h in hablo and haces is silent.

items.meaning: In the morning, I have breakfast.
items.note: Pause after mañana and keep the final o clear.
Stress the final syllable of después.
Let mediodía flow as one word.

items.meaning: In the afternoon, I practice Spanish.
items.note: Use a hard k sound in practico.
Finish the final o; do not swallow it.
Keep the h silent and let the question rise gently.

speaking.instructions: Carlos shares his routine, then helps you describe your own day one part at a time.

items.label: Listen
items.cue: Hear the time phrase and action as one thought, then repeat it.
items.meaning: In the morning, I have breakfast.
items.focus: Use a short pause after the time phrase.

items.label: Your turn
items.cue: Use después to move naturally to the next action.
items.focus: Make después sound like a connector, not a separate answer.

items.label: Keep talking
items.cue: Say that you eat at home before opening the model.
items.meaning: At midday, I eat at home.

items.label: One more challenge
items.cue: Join two evening actions with y.
items.prompt: Say that you have dinner and rest.
items.text: Por la noche, ceno y descanso.
items.meaning: At night, I have dinner and rest.
items.focus: Connect two yo forms with y.

items.label: Finish the conversation
items.cue: Describe four true parts of your day in order.
items.text: Por la mañana, estudio. Al mediodía, como. Por la tarde, trabajo. Por la noche, descanso.
items.meaning: In the morning, I study. At midday, I eat. In the afternoon, I work. At night, I rest.
items.focus: Change every action that is not true for you.

flashcards.selectionRule: Only Tier 1 time anchors, routine actions, and connectors required for the final description appear. The learner’s stated Spanish practice time appears first.

quiz.prompt: What does por la mañana mean?
quiz.explanation: Por la mañana introduces the morning part of a routine.
Al mediodía refers to the middle of the day.

quiz.prompt: What does descanso mean in this lesson?
quiz.explanation: Descanso is the first-person present form of descansar.
Después moves the routine from one action to the next.

quiz.prompt: Which sentence means I work in the morning?
quiz.explanation: Trabajo means I work and por la mañana means in the morning.
Use the first-person form desayuno when describing your own routine.
Comer becomes como for the first-person present.

quiz.prompt: Which sentence uses a regular yo form?
quiz.explanation: Practicar becomes practico when the speaker describes their own action.

options: the -o ending already identifies the speaker
yo is only used at night
quiz.answer: the -o ending already identifies the speaker
quiz.explanation: Trabajo already communicates I work, so Yo trabajo is optional unless emphasis is needed.
Normalmente means normally, como means I eat, and en casa means at home.

quiz.prompt: Translate: Por la noche, ceno con mi familia.
options: At night, I have dinner with my family.
In the afternoon, I walk with my family.
Every day, I study at home.
quiz.answer: At night, I have dinner with my family.
quiz.explanation: Ceno is I have dinner and por la noche is at night or in the evening.

quiz.prompt: Which sequence is in a natural order?
options: Primero desayuno. Después trabajo. Al final descanso.
Al final desayuno. Primero descanso. Después noche.
quiz.answer: Primero desayuno. Después trabajo. Al final descanso.
quiz.explanation: Primero opens the sequence, después continues it, and al final closes it.
For the regular first-person present here, remove -ar and add -o.

quiz.prompt: Which sentence is honest when an action is not daily?
options: A veces como fuera de casa.
Todos los días a veces siempre.
quiz.answer: A veces como fuera de casa.
quiz.explanation: A veces accurately softens the frequency to sometimes.

quiz.prompt: Carlos asks ¿Qué haces normalmente por la tarde? Answer naturally.
quiz.explanation: The response uses two first-person actions and directly answers the routine question.

quiz.prompt: Which description keeps one clear first-person perspective?
quiz.explanation: All four forms end in -o and consistently describe what I do.

quiz.prompt: What is the best reason to use normalmente instead of todos los días?
options: The action is usual but not guaranteed every day.
The speaker is asking a question.
The action happened in the past.
quiz.answer: The action is usual but not guaranteed every day.
quiz.explanation: Normalmente describes a typical habit without making an absolute claim.

quiz.prompt: Which response completes the four-part mission?
options: Por la mañana, desayuno. Al mediodía, como. Por la tarde, trabajo. Por la noche, descanso.
quiz.answer: Por la mañana, desayuno. Al mediodía, como. Por la tarde, trabajo. Por la noche, descanso.
quiz.explanation: It describes four ordered parts of a normal day with clear first-person actions.

miniConversation.title: Your day, not mine
miniConversation.setting: Carlos closes his notebook and asks the learner to describe a real routine without copying his.
miniConversation.goal: Answer one frequency question and describe morning, midday, afternoon, and night in order.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.english: What do you normally do in the morning?

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.spanish: Por la mañana, desayuno y trabajo.
turns.english: In the morning, I have breakfast and work.

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.english: At midday, I eat at home.

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.english: I practice Spanish in the afternoon.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.english: And how does your day end?

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.spanish: Por la noche, ceno y descanso.
turns.english: At night, I have dinner and rest.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.spanish: Excelente. Esa es tu rutina, contada por ti.
turns.english: Excellent. That is your routine, told by you.

culture.title: A day has local rhythms
culture.text: Daily schedules vary by family, job, and region. In Spain, lunch and dinner are often later than in Canada or the United States, and a long midday meal may be more important than a quick lunch. Do not treat one schedule as the only ‘Spanish’ routine. Ask what someone normally does, listen for their time phrases, and describe your own day honestly.

worldBuilding.carlosSpanish: Cuando llueve, Marta siempre pone la tetera aquí. Nico prefiere chocolate caliente.
worldBuilding.carlosEnglish: When it rains, Marta always puts the kettle here. Nico prefers hot chocolate.

worldBuilding.carlosSpanish: Preparo las clases en este rincón, pero las mejores ideas aparecen cuando camino por el barrio.
worldBuilding.carlosEnglish: I prepare classes in this corner, but the best ideas appear when I walk through the neighborhood.

worldBuilding.carlosSpanish: Desde el balcón se ve la calle otra vez. Madrid vuelve a moverse después de la lluvia.
worldBuilding.carlosEnglish: From the balcony you can see the street again. Madrid starts moving again after the rain.

livingWorldInteractions.title: Nico’s calendar drawing
livingWorldInteractions.prompt: A hand-drawn calendar is held to the refrigerator with a small magnet. Look closer.
livingWorldInteractions.carlosSpanish: Nico dibuja una estrella cuando cenamos todos juntos. Para él, ese es el mejor momento del día.
livingWorldInteractions.carlosEnglish: Nico draws a star when we all have dinner together. For him, that is the best part of the day.

nativeSpeech.note: A natural short way to return a routine question without repeating it.
Useful when a routine varies and the description is approximate.
Recognize this honest answer when someone does not have one fixed schedule.

commonMistakes.why: Use the first-person present form trabajo, not the infinitive trabajar.

commonMistakes.mistake: Yo trabajo, yo como, yo descanso.
commonMistakes.why: Spanish usually omits repeated yo because each -o ending already identifies the speaker.
Mañana is feminine when it means morning: la mañana.

commonMistakes.mistake: Todos los días for something occasional
commonMistakes.why: Choose a frequency phrase that truthfully matches the routine.

realLifeMission.title: Carlos Challenge: Tell Me About Your Day
realLifeMission.mission: Describe one real action from your morning, midday, afternoon, and night. Connect the four parts in order and include when you normally practice Spanish.
successCriteria: Use four different parts of the day.
Use at least four first-person action forms.
Use después, luego, or al final to connect actions.
Include the learner’s genuine Spanish practice time.
Keep the routine personal rather than copying Carlos word for word.
Complete the description without switching subjects.
realLifeMission.completionResponse: ¡Excelente! You just described an ordinary day in your own life. Spanish is no longer only happening around Carlos—it is describing you.
realLifeMission.correctionRule: Celebrate the complete routine first. Correct only one verb form or sequence phrase that made the timeline unclear.
realLifeMission.handoff: La lluvia termina. Carlos proposes one complete Madrid day where the learner will make the decisions.

canDo: I can organize a day from morning to night.
I can use a small set of first-person present forms.
I can say when I normally practice Spanish.
I can describe four real parts of my routine.
selfCheck: Can I use -o forms without saying yo every time?
Can I connect four actions in a clear order?
Can I distinguish normalmente from todos los días?
Can I describe my own day rather than Carlos’s?

passportStamp.title: A Day in Spanish
passportStamp.description: You turned an ordinary morning-to-night routine into your own connected Spanish story.
passportStamp.unlockCondition: Complete the Carlos Challenge for Lesson 9.

achievement.title: Daily Speaker
achievement.description: Describe four real parts of your normal day in Spanish.

carlosClosing: You just described an ordinary day in your own life, in order, using Spanish that belongs to you. The rain has stopped—tomorrow I want you to lead one complete day through Madrid.
